lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Do you know, sirrah, where Lieutenant Cassio lies?

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: I dare not say he lies any where.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, man?

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: He’s a soldier, and for one to say a soldier lies, is stabbing.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Go to: where lodges he?

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: To tell you where he lodges, is to tell you where I lie.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Can any thing be made of this?

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: I know not where he lodges, and for me to devise a

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: lodging and say he lies here or he lies there, were to

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: lie in mine own throat.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Can you inquire him out, and be edified by report?

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: I will catechise the world for him; that is, make questions, and by them answer.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Seek him, bid him come hither: tell him I have moved my

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: lord on his behalf, and hope all will be well.

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: To do this is within the compass of man’s wit: and therefore

lines.speaker: CLOWN
lines.text: I will attempt the doing it.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Where should I lose that handkerchief, Emilia?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I know not, madam.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Believe me, I had rather have lost my purse

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Full of crusadoes: and, but my noble Moor

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Is true of mind and made of no such baseness

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: As jealous creatures are, it were enough

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To put him to ill thinking.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Is he not jealous?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Who, he? I think the sun where he was born

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Drew all such humours from him.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Look, where he comes.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I will not leave him now till Cassio

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Be call’d to him.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Well, my good lady.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, hardness to dissemble!—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How do you, Desdemona?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Well, my good lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Give me your hand: this hand is moist, my lady.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: It yet hath felt no age nor known no sorrow.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: This argues fruitfulness and liberal heart:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Hot, hot, and moist: this hand of yours requires

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A sequester from liberty, fasting and prayer,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Much castigation, exercise devout;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For here’s a young and sweating devil here,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That commonly rebels. ’Tis a good hand,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A frank one.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: You may, indeed, say so;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: For ’twas that hand that gave away my heart.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A liberal hand: the hearts of old gave hands;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But our new heraldry is hands, not hearts.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I cannot speak of this. Come now, your promise.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What promise, chuck?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I have sent to bid Cassio come speak with you.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I have a salt and sorry rheum offends me;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Lend me thy handkerchief.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Here, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That which I gave you.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I have it not about me.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: No, indeed, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That is a fault.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That handkerchief

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Did an Egyptian to my mother give;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She was a charmer, and could almost read

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The thoughts of people: she told her, while

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: she kept it,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Twould make her amiable and subdue my father

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Entirely to her love, but if she lost it

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Or made gift of it, my father’s eye

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Should hold her loathed and his spirits should hunt

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: After new fancies: she, dying, gave it me;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And bid me, when my fate would have me wive,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To give it her. I did so: and take heed on’t;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Make it a darling like your precious eye;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To lose’t or give’t away were such perdition

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As nothing else could match.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Is’t possible?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis true: there’s magic in the web of it:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A sibyl, that had number’d in the world

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The sun to course two hundred compasses,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In her prophetic fury sew’d the work;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The worms were hallow’d that did breed the silk;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And it was dyed in mummy which the skilful

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Conserved of maidens’ hearts.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Indeed! is’t true?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Most veritable; therefore look to’t well.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Then would to God that I had never seen’t!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ha! wherefore?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why do you speak so startingly and rash?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Is’t lost? is’t gone? speak, is it out o’ the way?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Heaven bless us!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Say you?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: It is not lost; but what an if it were?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I say, it is not lost.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Fetch’t, let me see’t.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, so I can, sir, but I will not now.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: This is a trick to put me from my suit:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Pray you, let Cassio be received again.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Fetch me the handkerchief: my mind misgives.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Come, come;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: You’ll never meet a more sufficient man.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The handkerchief!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I pray, talk me of Cassio.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The handkerchief!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: A man that all his time

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Hath founded his good fortunes on your love,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Shared dangers with you—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The handkerchief!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: In sooth, you are to blame.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Away!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Is not this man jealous?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I ne’er saw this before.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Sure, there’s some wonder in this handkerchief:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I am most unhappy in the loss of it.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: ’Tis not a year or two shows us a man:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: They are all but stomachs, and we all but food;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: To eat us hungerly, and when they are full,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: They belch us. Look you, Cassio and my husband!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: There is no other way; ’tis she must do’t:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And, lo, the happiness! go, and importune her.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: How now, good Cassio! what’s the news with you?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Madam, my former suit: I do beseech you

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That by your virtuous means I may again

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Exist, and be a member of his love

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Whom I with all the office of my heart

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Entirely honour: I would not be delay’d.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: If my offence be of such mortal kind

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That nor my service past, nor present sorrows,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Nor purposed merit in futurity,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Can ransom me into his love again,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: But to know so must be my benefit;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: So shall I clothe me in a forced content,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And shut myself up in some other course,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: To fortune’s alms.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas, thrice-gentle Cassio!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My advocation is not now in tune;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My lord is not my lord; nor should I know him,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Were he in favour as in humour alter’d.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: So help me every spirit sanctified,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: As I have spoken for you all my best

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And stood within the blank of his displeasure

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: For my free speech! you must awhile be patient:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What I can do I will; and more I will

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Than for myself I dare: let that suffice you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is my lord angry?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: He went hence but now,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And certainly in strange unquietness.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Can he be angry? I have seen the cannon,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: When it hath blown his ranks into the air,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And, like the devil, from his very arm

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Puff’d his own brother:—and can he be angry?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Something of moment then: I will go meet him:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: There’s matter in’t indeed, if he be angry.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I prithee, do so.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Something, sure, of state,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Either from Venice, or some unhatch’d practise

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Made demonstrable here in Cyprus to him,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Hath puddled his clear spirit: and in such cases

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Men’s natures wrangle with inferior things,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Though great ones are their object. ’Tis even so;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: For let our finger ache, and it indues

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Our other healthful members even to that sense

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Of pain: nay, we must think men are not gods,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Nor of them look for such observances

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: As fit the bridal. Beshrew me much, Emilia,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I was, unhandsome warrior as I am,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Arraigning his unkindness with my soul;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But now I find I had suborn’d the witness,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And he’s indicted falsely.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Pray heaven it be state-matters, as you think,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And no conception nor no jealous toy

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Concerning you.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas the day! I never gave him cause.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: But jealous souls will not be answer’d so;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: They are not ever jealous for the cause,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: But jealous for they are jealous: ’tis a monster

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Begot upon itself, born on itself.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Heaven keep that monster from Othello’s mind!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Lady, amen.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I will go seek him. Cassio, walk hereabout:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If I do find him fit, I’ll move your suit

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And seek to effect it to my uttermost.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I humbly thank your ladyship.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Save you, friend Cassio!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: What make you from home?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: How is it with you, my most fair Bianca?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I’ faith, sweet love, I was coming to your house.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: And I was going to your lodging, Cassio.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: What, keep a week away? seven days and nights?

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Eight score eight hours? and lovers’ absent hours,

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: More tedious than the dial eight score times?

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: O weary reckoning!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Pardon me, Bianca:

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I have this while with leaden thoughts been press’d:

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: But I shall, in a more continuate time,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Strike off this score of absence. Sweet Bianca,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Take me this work out.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: O Cassio, whence came this?

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: This is some token from a newer friend:

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: To the felt absence now I feel a cause:

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Is’t come to this? Well, well.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Go to, woman!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Throw your vile guesses in the devil’s teeth,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: From whence you have them. You are jealous now

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That this is from some mistress, some remembrance:

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: No, in good troth, Bianca.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Why, whose is it?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I know not, sweet: I found it in my chamber.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I like the work well: ere it be demanded—

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: As like enough it will—I’ld have it copied:

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Take it, and do’t; and leave me for this time.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Leave you! wherefore?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I do attend here on the general;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And think it no addition, nor my wish,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: To have him see me woman’d.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: Why, I pray you?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Not that I love you not.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: But that you do not love me.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: I pray you, bring me on the way a little,

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: And say if I shall see you soon at night.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: ’Tis but a little way that I can bring you;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: For I attend here: but I’ll see you soon.

lines.speaker: BIANCA
lines.text: ’Tis very good; I must be circumstanced.

stage_directions.text: How is’t with you, my lord?

glosses.definition: a form of address to a male inferior or servant
lodges — the Clown quibbles that 'lies' means speaks falsely
Portuguese gold coins stamped with the cross
temperamental moods; bodily fluids believed to govern character
difficulty in concealing what I feel
in palmistry, a moist palm signaled a passionate, possibly lustful, nature
shows fertility and a too-generous disposition — Othello's veiled accusation
free giving; by implication, sexual looseness
gypsy; one believed in Shakespeare's day to be from Egypt and skilled in enchantment
a female prophet of pagan antiquity
a pigment made from embalmed bodies, used as a medicine and dye
unworthy soldier — Desdemona turns Othello's earlier endearment on herself
subject to circumstances; obliged to wait

annotations.title: The handkerchief and its contradictions
annotations.body:
Othello's account of the handkerchief's origin is a Renaissance marvel: an Egyptian charmer, a sibyl in prophetic frenzy, silkworms at the full moon, dye made of mummy (powdered embalmed bodies) conserved from maidens' hearts. Every detail is excessive. The handkerchief is love-token, ethnic heirloom, magical object, theatrical prop, forensic evidence. It is made to bear more meaning than any small cloth can bear.

Shakespeare is deliberate about this. The handkerchief has, across the play, already meant fidelity (a wedding token), matriarchal inheritance (Othello's mother's), ordinary housekeeping (it wipes Othello's brow), erotic transfer (handed to Cassio through Emilia's theft), and commodity (borrowed by Bianca). It survives each meaning and acquires the next. By Act 5 it will have outlived its interpretive anchor: Othello will read it as proof of adultery, Desdemona as a kept kindness, Emilia as a husband's trinket, Cassio as an unknown gift. No one reading is wrong; each is the handkerchief for its holder.

This is the play's most sustained meditation on evidence. The cloth itself stays the same. The meaning it bears is entirely a function of who holds it and what they need it to mean.
sources: Lynda E. Boose, 'Othello's Handkerchief', *English Literary Renaissance* (1975)
Harry Berger Jr., *Making Trifles of Terrors* (1997)
James L. Calderwood, *The Properties of Othello* (1989)

annotations.title: Bianca and the three-woman structure
annotations.body:
Bianca's entrance introduces the play's third female type. Desdemona is the wife; Emilia is the maid and the ironic realist; Bianca is the mistress, the professional companion whom Cassio is honored to receive and ashamed to be seen with. The triad — wife, servant, courtesan — maps the range of women Venetian society acknowledged, and the traffic in women between them, figured by the handkerchief moving from Desdemona's hand to Emilia's to Iago's to Cassio's to Bianca's, is a slow demonstration of how female property is passed and reread.

Bianca's rage at the handkerchief's provenance — 'I must take out the work?' — is the first honest anger any woman voices in the act. The play will, by 5.2, return to Emilia the same capacity. The sequence matters: the women closest to the center of power speak last.
sources: Carol Thomas Neely, *Broken Nuptials in Shakespeare's Plays* (1985)
Ann Jennalie Cook, *Making a Match: Courtship in Shakespeare and His Society* (1991)

trials.prompt: According to Othello, where did the handkerchief originally come from?
options: A Venetian jeweler commissioned by his father
An Egyptian charmer/sibyl who gave it to his mother
The Byzantine emperor's personal gift to Venetian allies
A gift from Brabantio to welcome him into the family

trials.prompt: Desdemona lies to Othello about the handkerchief — she says she is not without it, though she has lost it. Why?
options: She is beginning an affair with Cassio and wants to cover her tracks
She is trying to protect Othello from upset while she searches for it, a well-intentioned evasion that will turn ruinous
She has given it to Cassio as a love token
She has sold it to pay a gambling debt

trials.prompt: The handkerchief means different things to different characters (wedding token, maternal heirloom, magical object, romantic prop, cheap cloth). What does Shakespeare's handling of this object suggest about evidence?
options: Evidence is always objective and speaks for itself
Meaning is not in the object but in who holds it and what they need it to mean
Handkerchiefs should be registered with Venetian authorities
Only the original owner can correctly interpret an object's meaning

trials.prompt: What does Othello mean when he tells Desdemona her palm is 'moist'?
options: She has been working with water
In Elizabethan palmistry, a moist palm suggested a passionate, possibly lustful nature — a veiled accusation
She is ill and should rest
It is a gesture of affection and warmth
